preFix.title: Pinch-pot cactus planter trio
preFix.subtitle: Three hand-pinched planters in squat, tall, and flared bowl shapes
preFix.excerpt: Three small pinch-pot planters shaped by hand from air-dry clay: a squat round pot, a tall cylinder, and a shallow flared bowl. Each has a drainage hole pressed into the base before drying. PVA sealing improves water resistance for small succulents and cacti. No kiln is needed.
preFix.sourceNotes: Technique derived from first principles. Encyclopedia Britannica 11th edition (1911) pottery entry consulted for historical context on pinch-building methods.

content.text: This project uses the
pinch-pot

technique to produce three planters from air-dry clay: a squat round pot (50 mm tall, 70 mm wide), a tall cylinder (80 mm tall, 50 mm diameter), and a shallow flared bowl (40 mm tall, 80 mm wide). A drainage hole is pressed into each base before drying. No kiln is required. PVA sealing applied to the interior and exterior gives enough resistance for small succulents and cacti planted in a well-draining substrate.
Preparation
Knead each ball of clay for two minutes before pinching. Kneading drives out air pockets that would otherwise cause the wall to crack during drying. Aim for a
wall thickness

of 6-8 mm across all three pots. Walls thinner than 5 mm are fragile once dry; walls thicker than 10 mm take much longer to dry evenly and are prone to surface cracking. Keep a small bowl of water nearby to dampen your fingers if the clay surface begins to dry while you are pinching.
Construction

Make the squat round pot. Roll 120 g of clay into a ball. Press your thumb into the centre to a depth of 30 mm, leaving approximately 10 mm of base below your thumb tip. Begin pinching: rotate the ball in your non-dominant hand while your dominant thumb inside and fingers outside squeeze the wall in a steady rhythm. Work upward in passes from the base to the rim, thinning the wall to 6-8 mm as you go. The finished pot should be 50 mm tall and 70 mm wide across the opening. Set on the board and flatten the base gently by pressing it against the board surface.

Make the tall cylinder. Roll 100 g of clay into a ball. Press your thumb in as before, but this time work the wall upward without widening the opening, keeping the sides nearly vertical. Aim for 80 mm tall and 50 mm diameter. This form requires more passes than the squat pot; resist the urge to thin the walls in one pass as the form will collapse. Work incrementally: two pinching passes to thin, then rotate and assess.

Make the shallow flared bowl. Roll 130 g of clay into a ball. Press your thumb in to 20 mm depth, then pinch the wall while also pressing outward with your thumb to flare the form. The finished bowl should be 40 mm tall and 80 mm wide, with the rim noticeably wider than the base. This is the widest and shallowest of the three forms.

Pierce the drainage hole in each pot's base. While the clay is still soft, push the needle tool through the centre of the base of each pot to create a hole approximately 6 mm in diameter. Widen it by rotating the needle tool. This hole allows excess water to drain when the planter is in use.

Smooth the rims. Dampen the tip of your finger or use a pottery sponge to smooth the top edge of each pot. Remove any fingerprint ridges from the rim. For a more finished look, use the fettling knife to trim the rim level by cutting around the circumference at a consistent height.

Add surface texture while the clay is soft. Press a twisted rope, a seed pod, a stamp, or a fingernail pattern into the exterior of each pot. This is the last opportunity to texture the surface before the clay stiffens to the
leather-hard
stage.
Drying
Place all three pots upright on a wooden board at room temperature. Allow 24-36 hours to reach the leather-hard stage, then turn each pot upside down to allow the base to dry evenly. Once the
bone-dry
stage is reached (usually 48-3 days total) the clay will appear uniformly pale and feel room temperature. At the
greenware
stage all three pots are fragile and will dissolve if wetted before sealing. Handle them with care and do not stack them.
Finishing

Paint the exterior of each pot with acrylic paint if desired; allow to dry for at least two hours. Apply two coats of PVA diluted 50:50 with water to the interior and exterior of each pot, including around the drainage hole. Apply the interior coat first using a small brush pushed down into the pot. Let each coat dry completely before adding the next. The sealed interior extends the life of the pot when used with a well-draining succulent compost.
Care

Air-dry clay is not waterproof even after PVA sealing. These pots are not suitable as water vessels and should be used only with plants that require infrequent watering, such as cacti and succulents. Place a saucer beneath each pot and keep indoors. Water sparingly to prevent saturation of the clay walls. Do not leave standing in water. Wipe the exterior with a dry cloth.
Variations
Grouped set on a tray: make five or six pots in varied sizes using the same three shapes and arrange them on a wooden tray or piece of slate as a cohesive display.
Colour-dipped effect: paint the lower two-thirds of each pot in a terracotta or sand colour. Paint the upper third in a contrasting tone. This creates a dipped-glaze look without any actual glaze.

Added coil foot ring: attach a thin coil as a foot ring around the base of each pot at the leather-hard stage using score-and-slip. This raises the pot slightly, prevents water from pooling around the base, and gives a more finished appearance.

rows.fix: Dampen your fingers lightly and smooth the crack closed. Work more quickly on each pass to prevent surface drying, or lightly mist the outer surface of the pot with water from a spray bottle
rows.cause: The clay has dried on the surface during the pinching process

rows.fix: At the early drying stage, gently push the walls outward with your hand. If collapse is severe, rebuild from fresh clay
rows.cause: Walls were thinned below 5 mm or the clay was too wet when set down

rows.fix: At the leather-hard stage, push the needle tool back through to reopen the hole; widen to at least 5 mm diameter
rows.cause: The hole was pierced too small or the base clay sagged

rows.fix: At the leather-hard stage, press the base gently against a flat surface. If fully dry, sand the base lightly with medium-grit sandpaper
rows.cause: The base was not flattened against the board after forming
rows.problem: Base is uneven and the pot rocks
